challenges.situation: Ever since your boss called your launch deck 'brilliant' in front of leadership, you find yourself volunteering for visible, demo-able work and quietly dropping the unglamorous maintenance no one watches.

options.text: Decide praise is hollow, stop seeking it, and ignore feedback on principle.
options.feedback: Total imperviousness is just a different deafness. Praise carries real signal about what's landing — refusing all of it throws out useful information.

options.text: Write down what 'good work' means by your own standard, and weigh tasks against that, not the applause.
options.feedback: Defining good before you're judged keeps your direction anchored inside. Praise can then inform you without governing what you choose to do.

options.text: Lean into the visible work — the recognition confirms it's where you add the most value.
options.feedback: You've let applause become the scorecard. Steering by 'what gets noticed' instead of 'what matters' hands the wheel to whoever grants the praise.

challenges.situation: A strong engineer ships a solid fix, then keeps refreshing the channel, visibly deflating when the thread stays quiet, unable to move on to the next task.
challenges.question: What's behind the inability to let it go?

options.text: External validation propping up his sense of worth.
options.feedback: When the doing isn't enough without the seeing, the hook is in. If worth wasn't secured from inside, praise isn't a bonus — it's oxygen.
Healthy pride is quiet and self-contained — 'I did this well, I know it.' The anxious scanning for a reaction is its opposite.

options.text: Reasonable interest in whether the fix worked.
options.feedback: Interest in the outcome could be checked once and dropped. The refreshing and deflating is about a reaction, not the result.

challenges.front: Why does praise that comes only sometimes hook you harder than praise that comes every time?
challenges.back: Intermittent reinforcement. Reliable rewards lose their pull; unpredictable ones make the brain keep pulling the lever, hoping this time's the hit. It's the same variable-ratio pattern that makes slot machines and feeds compulsive — randomness, not generosity, builds the addiction.

challenges.context: A monkey is lured onto a crocodile's back by a feast thrown 'in his honor,' then told mid-river the real plan is to eat his heart.

scenarioA.label: Flattered
scenarioA.text: Glows at the honor, climbs aboard without a question, ends up helpless in deep water.

scenarioB.label: Awake to the bait
scenarioB.text: Snaps alert mid-river, sees the flattery for what it is, and talks his way back to the tree.

challenges.explanation: What saved the monkey wasn't refusing praise — it was waking up to what the praise was for. As Epictetus warned, when your peace depends on approval, you've handed over the controls.

principle.text: Praise is a fine reward and a terrible master.
principle.subtext: When approval becomes the fuel your identity runs on, you stop steering by 'what's true' and start steering by 'what gets applause' — and whoever controls the applause now controls you.

reflection: List whose approval you find yourself working for. Now ask of your current work: would I still be proud of this if no one ever noticed it?
